questions.questionText:

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct and both of them explain Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct, but only one of them explains Statement–I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements–II and III is

questions.questionText:
Minamata
Convention
Reduce the anthropogenic
emission of Mercury
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The National Green Tribunal (NGT)** was established under the NGT Act, 2010, for the speedy and effective resolution of environmental disputes, including cases related to forest conservation, natural resource protection, and environmental rights.
● The **Tribunal has the powers of a civil court** under the Code of Civil Procedure to perform its functions, but it can frame its own rules. It ensures speedy environmental justice and helps ease the caseload of higher courts.
● The NGT **adjudicates civil cases** under seven major environmental laws, including the Water, Air, Forest Conservation, and Environment Protection Acts. However, it does **not have jurisdiction over the** **Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972 and the Forest Rights Act,** 2006. **So, Statement I is correct.**
● The Tribunal has jurisdiction over all civil cases that involve substantial environmental questions. Furthermore, any person aggrieved by an order or direction issued by an Appellate Authority under the relevant environmental laws **can appeal to the National Green Tribunal.** The decisions of **the Tribunal** **are binding**. The Tribunal’s orders are enforceable as the powers vested are the same as in a civil court under the **Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.** The Tribunal has the authority to review its own decisions. If a review is unsuccessful, the decision may be **appealed before the Supreme Court** within ninety days. **So, Statement II is not correct.**
● The National Green Tribunal (NGT) has five regional branches, with the Principal Bench in New Delhi and other four benches located in **Bhopal (Central Zone), Pune (West Zone), Kolkata (East Zone),** **and Chennai (South Zone).** These branches are responsible for handling cases within their specified geographical jurisdiction and can hold circuit sittings in other locations to increase accessibility. **So,** **Statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Pollution Related (E-Waste, Solid Waste, Pollutants, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Basel
Convention
Ensure the sustainable
management of electronic
waste
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ozone (O₃)** is a **naturally occurring gas formed when sunlight splits molecular oxygen (O₂) into** **single atoms that then combine with O₂.** It is thermodynamically unstable and breaks down easily, but under natural conditions, its formation and destruction stay in balance. **Ozone depletion** happens when this equilibrium is disturbed, leading to more breakdown than formation.
● **The ozone hole** is a result of the disequilibrium caused by **interaction between** ozone depleting substances**, sunlight radiation, stratospheric clouds, and the extreme cold and wind speeds of** **the polar vortex**. The ozone layer is primarily destroyed through reactions with molecules **containing** **nitrogen, hydrogen, chlorine, or bromine**. The human-made chemicals **like chlorofluorocarbons** **(CFCs), release chlorine and bromine** into the atmosphere, catalysing the destruction of ozone molecules.
● **Role of Sunlight Radiation:** o In the polar winter CFCs and halons get accumulated in the stratosphere and remain in stable, inactive forms. When **sunlight (UV radiation) returns in summer**, it breaks these stable compounds apart, releasing reactive halogen species mainly chlorine monoxide (ClO) and bromine monoxide (BrO) which then drive the chemical reactions that destroy ozone. **So, Statement II is** **correct.**
● **Role of Temperature and Stratospheric Clouds:** o Ozone depletion is directly related to stratospheric temperature. When **temperatures drop below** **–78°C, Polar Stratospheric Clouds** (PSCs) form. PSCs play a crucial role in converting inactive chlorine (from CFCs and other man-made compounds) into reactive forms that **destroy ozone.** Reactions on liquid and solid PSC particles cause the highly reactive chlorine gas ClO to be formed, which catalytically destroys ozone.
● **Ozone Depletion in Antarctica is very prominent than over Arctic:** o In Antarctica, **these low temperatures persist longer**, **allowing more PSCs to form**. In contrast, the Arctic has more variable weather and warmer stratospheric winters, so PSC formation is less consistent. The range of winter minimum temperatures found in the **Arctic is much greater** than that in the Antarctic. o **Example:** In winter, the lower stratosphere averages around −92°C over Antarctica (July–August) and −80°C, over the Arctic (December–January). Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs) form below −78°C, lasting about 5 months in Antarctica but only 1–2 months in the Arctic. o Therefore, although depletion of stratospheric ozone occurs over both hemispheres of the Earth, this phenomenon is more pronounced in the Southern Hemisphere (Antarctica) than in the Northern Hemisphere (Arctic). **So, Statement I is correct.** **So,** Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The 7th longest-lived ozone hole on record, Antarctic ozone hole closed in the year 2023.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Bonn
Convention
Conservation of migratory
birds
Which of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Greenhouse gases** absorb and quickly **re-emit infrared radiation**, **disrupting the direct escape of heat** from Earth back into space and **causing warming of earth**. Their ability to do this **depends on the energy** **levels of electrons** within the molecules—particularly the shared electrons in covalent bonds between atoms of differing electronegativities, which create dipole moments.
● Methane (CH₄) has a much higher Global Warming Potential than carbon dioxide (CO₂) — about 28 times greater over 100 years, and over 80 times greater over 20 years. **So, Statement I is correct.**
● **Factors Responsible for Higher Global Warming Potential of Methane:** o **Molecular Geometry:**
▪ Methane consists of a central carbon atom bonded to four hydrogen atoms, forming a tetrahedral structure while CO₂ is linear as it has a central carbon atom with just two oxygen atoms attached to it. ▪ The **tetrahedral shape allows methane to absorb infrared radiation** across a broader range of frequencies, enhancing its heat-trapping efficiency. **So, Statement III is correct.** o **Concentration and Diminishing Returns:**
▪ Methane is present in the atmosphere at around 1,900 parts per billion (ppb), whereas CO₂ is much more abundant at approximately 416 parts per million (ppm)—over 200 times more concentrated. The first 20 ppm of CO₂ accounts for **roughly one-third of its total** **warming effect.** ▪ Beyond that, each additional ppm of CO₂ has a diminishing warming impact because **it** **absorbs radiation in bands that are already saturated**. In contrast, **methane exists in** **much lower concentrations and operates on the steeper part of the radiative forcing** **curve**, meaning small increases in methane concentration result in disproportionately large warming effects.
● **Life Span of Methane Molecule:** o A molecule of methane traps more heat than a molecule of CO2, **but methane has a relatively** **short lifespan of 7 to 12 years in the atmosphere,** while CO2 can persist for hundreds of years or more. o It is because, methane is primarily removed from the atmosphere through chemical reactions, specifically with hydroxyl radicals (OH). These reactions occur in the troposphere, the lowest part of the atmosphere, and convert methane into carbon dioxide and water. **So, Statement** **II is not correct.** **So, Only one of the Statements–II and III is correct and that explains Statement–I** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Sulphur Dioxide:
I. - Its largest sources are agriculture and decomposing landfill waste.
II. 	 It oxidizes in the presence of water vapour to
form sulphuric acid, which contributes to the formation of PM2.5.
III. 	 India is the largest emitter of sulphur dioxide in
the world.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sulphur dioxide (SO****2****) is a colourless**, reactive air pollutant with a strong odour. It is released into the atmosphere from both **natural sources such as volcanic eruptions** and human activities like fossil fuel combustion, metal smelting, and chemical manufacturing. It **is a key air pollutant** and is considered critical for monitoring under various air quality guidelines and regulatory frameworks, including those set by the World Health Organization (WHO).
● **Sources of Sulphur dioxide:** It is released into the atmosphere from both anthropogenic and natural sources as discussed below.
o One of the primary human-related sources is the **burning of fossil fuels such as coal and oil** for domestic heating, power generation, transportation (including locomotives and ships), and other industrial activities. Another **major source is the smelting of mineral ores** that contain sulphur, such as iron pyrite and copper pyrite, which **releases SO₂ during the extraction process**. In addition to these, **natural volcanic activity** is a significant contributor, as volcanoes emit large quantities of SO₂ during eruptions. **So, Statement I is not correct.** o **Agriculture** is the dominant anthropogenic source of ammonia (NH₃), while **composting and** **landfills** are significant sources of methane (CH₄) and nitrous oxide (N₂O).
● **Impact of Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) on the Environment:** o Sulphur Dioxide contributes **significantly to air pollution** through its **chemical transformations** in the atmosphere: o It **oxidizes in the presence of water vapour to form sulphuric acid** (H₂SO₄), which then contributes to **the formation of secondary particulate matter (PM2.5). So, Statement II is** **correct.**
● **India is the largest emitter** of sulphur dioxide (SO₂) in the world followed by Russia, contributing over 21% of global SO₂ emissions. **The primary source is coal-based electricity generation,** driven by the country’s heavy reliance on thermal power plants. Several locations in India like **Singrauli, Neyveli,** **Talcher, Jharsuguda, and Korba,** are major SO2 emission hotspots. **So, Statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements: **Statement-I:** River interlinking projects can lead to disruptions in the rainfall distribution. **Statement-II:** River interlinking alters soil moisture and irrigation patterns, thereby modifying evapotranspiration rates.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **River interlinking projects** involve transferring water from surplus to deficit river basins via reservoirs and canals. While the objective is to address water scarcity, such large-scale redistribution can lead to **significant hydro-meteorological impacts**, including **disruption in the spatial distribution of** **monsoon rainfall**. **So, Statement I is correct.**
● A **considerable portion of monsoon rainfall is derived from recycled precipitation**—moisture that evaporates from land, vegetation, and irrigation systems and then precipitates again within the region. In India, the **Himalayan mountains** and low-pressure systems over the **Bay of Bengal** are key drivers of precipitation recycling. **West Central India** acts as a major source of moisture for recycled rainfall, whereas Northeast India receives a significant portion of this recycled precipitation.
● River interlinking alters **soil moisture and irrigation patterns**, thereby **modifying evapotranspiration** **rates**, which are key to this recycling process. These changes disrupt the **land–atmosphere feedback** **mechanisms**, ultimately affecting **rainfall distribution across interconnected and even distant basins**. **So, Statement II is correct.**
● Interlinking may **hamper the environmental flow of rivers to the oceans**, reducing freshwater discharge. This can **alter the land–ocean interaction**, affect coastal salinity and atmospheric dynamics, and in turn **influence monsoon behaviour**. Furthermore, reduced downstream flow also impacts **regional** **atmospheric moisture availability**, which can exacerbate rainfall variability and water stress in certain regions. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** River interlinking alters land-atmosphere feedback and changes the Indian summer monsoon.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biogeochemical Cycles (C, N, S, P, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Carbon Spectrum
Description
I - Red carbon
Carbon released due to deposition of biological particles on snow
II. - Brown carbon
Carbon released by the incomplete combustion of organic matter
III. - Teal carbon
Carbon stored in freshwater and wetland environments
IV. - Purple carbon
Carbon stored in ocean plants and sediments
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The 'Carbon Rainbow'** is a conceptual model that classifies carbon into various colours as red, black, blue etc., according to its origin, position in the environment, and role within the carbon cycle.
● **The different carbon includes:**
o **Purple**: Carbon **captured through the air or industrial emissions** and reused in the production of various goods. By reducing the overall carbon footprint, it not only helps mitigate the impact of greenhouse gases on global warming but also supports the creation of sustainable products**. So,** **pair IV is not correctly matched.** o **Teal: Teal carbon is the amount of carbon stored in inland freshwater wetlands.** Teal carbon ecosystems are shallow inland wetlands (non-tidal), making them different from blue carbon in terms of their shallow water depth, high hydro-climatic sensitivity, and absence of tidal influence for salinity and nutrient loads So, pair III is correctly matched. o **Brown**: Carbon **released by the incomplete combustion of organic matter.** In comparison with black carbon, which is often described as graphitic and absorbing a wide spectrum of light, brown carbon is the visible- and UV-light-absorbing component of organic aerosols. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.** o **Red**: Carbon **released through biological particles on snow and ice** that reduces albedo. It is the newest color in the carbon spectrum. These particles, often microorganisms, produce pigments that absorb sunlight, leading to increased warming and faster melting of snow and ice, which in turn releases nutrients trapped within the ice crystals. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Hazardous, Radioactive, E-Waste, Heavy Metals, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Disease Caused due to
I - Itai-Itai Cadmium
II. - Blue baby syndrome Nitrates
III. - Plumbism Lead
IV. - Acrodynia Mercury
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Heavy metals** are dense metallic elements that occur naturally but have seen increased environmental release due to industrial activities, mining, and fossil fuel use. They accumulate in soil, water, and food chains, leading to **bioaccumulation**, **biomagnification**, and serious health risks like cardiovascular, neurological, and reproductive disorders.
● Diseases caused by Heavy metals: o **Cadmium:**
▪ Cadmium is a **highly toxic, non-essential heavy metal** used in electroplating, batteries, and as a protective **coating due to its corrosion resistance**. Human exposure occurs mainly via contaminated food (like rice and shellfish) and tobacco smoke. ▪ **Itai-itai disease is the most severe case of chronic cadmium** (Cd) toxicity, resulting in osteomalacia, severe pain throughout the body (“itai” means “ouch”), and bone deformities and fractures. **So, pair I is correctly matched.** o **Nitrates:**
▪ **Nitrates** are commonly used in **fertilizers**, and their contamination in **groundwater** is a growing concern, especially in agricultural regions.  Human exposure occurs primarily through **drinking nitrate-contaminated water** and consumption of **nitrate-rich** **vegetables**. ▪ In the body, nitrates can convert to **nitrites**, which interfere with the blood’s oxygencarrying capacity by forming **methemoglobin**. This condition, known as **methemoglobinemia** or “**blue baby syndrome**,” primarily affects infants, causing **cyanosis** and, in severe cases, death. **So, pair II is correctly matched.** o **Lead:**
▪ **Lead** has been extensively used in gasoline, paints, water pipelines, batteries, cooking utensils, construction materials, and the toy industry. Lead enters the body through inhalation or ingestion, especially in children, and accumulates in bones, blood, and soft tissues. ▪ It disrupts multiple body systems and is particularly harmful to the nervous system, leading to cognitive impairment, developmental delays, and **behavioural issues**, called **lead** **poisoning or Plumbism.** Chronic exposure also affects the **renal, cardiovascular, and** **reproductive systems**. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** o **Mercury:**
▪ **Mercury** is a **toxic heavy metal** used in **dental amalgams**, some **industrial processes**, and **electronic devices**. Once ingested, mercury accumulates in the **brain, kidneys, and** **liver**, affecting the **nervous system**, particularly in foetuses and young children. **Mercury** **poisoning** also causes **acrodynia**, a rare condition that leads to **pain, redness, and** **discoloration** in the hands and feet, especially in children. **So, pair IV is correctly** **matched.** **So, all the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Material Leached through
I - Dioxins Polyvinyl chloride
II. - Antimony Polyethylene terephthalate
III. - Styrene Polystyrene
IV. - Bisphenol A Polycarbonate
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Plastics are synthetic polymers** used extensively in packaging, consumer products, healthcare, construction, and electronics. Each plastic type is chemically distinct and can **leach toxic substances** under certain conditions—such as heat, UV exposure, or long-term storage, posing serious **environmental** **and human health risks**.
● **Polyvinyl Chloride:** o **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** is commonly used in toys, cling wraps, blister packs, detergent bottles, binders, blood bags, and medical tubing. Once the second most widely used plastic after polyethylene, PVC has since raised serious concerns due to its **toxic production and disposal** **processes**. o It contains a high proportion of **chlorine**, and during **incineration or high-heat processing**, it can release **dioxins**, one of the most toxic environmental pollutants. **Dioxins** are **persistent organic** **pollutants (POPs)** with long-term effects on the **immune system, reproductive health** and are linked to **cancer**. **So, Pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Polyethylene Terephthalate:** o **PET or PETE (Polyethylene Terephthalate)**, also known as polyester, is a wrinkle-resistant plastic widely used for food and beverage packaging due to its excellent barrier properties. **PET** is widely used for **bottled water, soft drinks, food containers**, and synthetic fibres. o **Antimony trioxide** is used as a **catalyst** during PET production and may leach, especially when **bottles are reused or heated**, especially when exposed to heat or used for long-term storage. o Antimony is a **heavy metal** linked to **respiratory irritation, gastrointestinal distress**, and is a **suspected carcinogen**. **So, Pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Polystyrene:** o **Polystyrene (PS)**, commonly known as **styrofoam**, is widely used in **food containers, egg** **cartons, disposable cups and bowls, packaging**, and **bike helmets**. o When in contact with **hot or oily food**, PS can leach **styrene**, a **neurotoxin** harmful to the **brain** **and nervous system**. o **Styrene** is a **probable human carcinogen** and a **neurotoxin**, linked to **central nervous system** **effects** and **hormonal disruption**. o It may also impact the lungs, liver, immune system, and even genes. Additionally, PS has a very low recycling rate, making it an environmental concern. **So, Pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Polycarbonate:** o **Polycarbonate** is used in **baby bottles, water jugs, eyewear, CDs, and electronics**. o It often contains **Bisphenol A (BPA)**, an **endocrine disruptor** that can leach into food or liquids, especially under heat or mechanical stress. o **BPA, found in polycarbonate (PC),** has been linked to numerous health issues, including hormonal disruption (such as early puberty and infertility), chromosomal and reproductive damage, neurological and behavioral disorders, and an increased risk of cancer, diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular diseases**. So, Pair IV is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Which of the following best describes the pseudo-
persistent pollutants?

options.a:
Biodegradable materials, whose rate of addition
of the pollutant is higher than its elimination rate from the environment
options.b:
Non-biodegradable pollutants that are rapidly
removed from the environment by natural processes
options.c:
Persistent organic pollutants that undergo
immediate photodegradation upon release in the environment
options.d:
Naturally occurring pollutants that never
accumulate in any ecosystem due to their high solubility in water

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pseudo-persistent pollutants** are substances that may be biodegradable, but their continuous or repeated input into the environment exceeds the rate at which natural systems can eliminate or degrade them. As a result, these pollutants accumulate in the environment, mimicking the behaviour of persistent pollutants despite their degradability.
● **They are generally synthetic organic chemicals** that possess relatively lower potential of environmental persistence, bioaccumulative, and toxic properties than those of classical POPs. Unlike POPs (e.g., dioxins, PCBs), pseudo-persistent pollutants can degrade, but chronic loading keeps their concentration high.
● Although PPOPs may not **bioaccumulate** to the same extent as classical POPs, PPOPs can still pose **toxicological risks**. They can contaminate **surface and groundwater**, disrupt **aquatic ecosystems**, and interfere with **biochemical processes** in non-target organisms.
● Continuous low-level exposure to such compounds has been linked to **hormonal disruption**, **antibiotic** **resistance**, and changes in the **reproductive or immune systems** of wildlife. Humans may be affected through **contaminated water or food chains**, especially when pharmaceuticals are involved.
● **Other Options:** o Pseudo-Persistent organic pollutants don't undergo immediate photodegradation upon release. Persistent organic pollutants (POPs), by definition, resist degradation, including photodegradation. Immediate photodegradation would mean they don’t persist, hence they cannot be pseudopersistent either. o Pseudo-persistent organic pollutants are not necessarily **naturally occurring**; many are **synthetic**, like **pharmaceuticals or surfactants**, pesticides etc. o A non-biodegradable pollutant, by definition, cannot be rapidly removed by natural processes like microbial action or photodegradation. Also, pseudo-persistence is about accumulation due to slow removal, not rapid elimination. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Which of the following conditions can lead to a
sulfur smog in an area?
I. - Reduced temperatures with long nights
II. 	 High relative humidity
III. 	 Presence of thermal power plants in the nearby
regions
IV. 	 Reduced incidence of sunlight
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Smog** is a visible, **polluted haze** that frequently blankets urban areas causing pollution and health impacts. **A sulphurous smog** is formed when sulphur oxide emissions, primarily **sulphur dioxide** from the burning of coal (and occasionally oil) **combine with water vapor** in the atmosphere. This reaction produces **sulfuric acid**, a harmful substance that not only poses serious health risks but also contributes to **acid** **deposition**, including **acid rain**.
● This acid has a **strong tendency to absorb additional water** from the atmosphere due to its **hygroscopic** nature. As a result, fog droplets **increase in size and density**, making the fog **thicker and more** **persistent**, and contributing significantly to the formation of **dense, choking smog**. **Sulphuric acid,** **sulphate aerosols, Particulate Matter (PM), and soot** function as secondary pollutants within this smog system. These are not emitted directly in their harmful form but are instead **formed through chemical** **reactions in the atmosphere**. Hence, this smog is considered one of the most severe forms of air pollution. **Some of the factors aiding the formation of sulphurous smog includes:**
● **Temperature inversion:**
o Under normal conditions, warm air rises, allowing pollutants to disperse. However, during a temperature inversion, a layer of warmer air traps cooler air near the ground. o This **prevents vertical air mixing**, causing pollutants like sulphur dioxide and soot to accumulate near the surface, thereby intensifying smog formation and making it persist for longer durations.
● **Radiation:** o At night, especially under clear skies and calm conditions, the Earth's surface **loses heat rapidly** through terrestrial (longwave) radiation. o The ground becomes cooler than the air above, resulting in a temperature inversion favouring smog.
● **High relative humidity:** o High humidity and fog **provide the necessary moisture for sulphur dioxide to dissolve** and undergo chemical transformations in atmospheric water droplets. o This leads to the formation of sulfuric acid and other secondary pollutants, which then combine with the fog to create thick, toxic smog**. So, point II is correct.**
● **Low temperature (< 35 degrees Fahrenheit):** o Cold **temperatures slow down the dispersion of pollutants** and enhance the condensation of water vapor into fog. These conditions also favour the formation of temperature inversions and reduce the efficiency of atmospheric cleansing mechanisms, allowing pollutants to accumulate and contribute to severe smog episodes. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Presence of thermal power plants in the nearby regions:** o Thermal power plants often burn coal and release sulphur dioxide (SO₂), the primary pollutant responsible for sulphurous smog. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Reduced incidence of sunlight:** o Unlike Photochemical smog, Sulphur smog is not driven by sunlight. It forms under cloudy, foggy conditions with little or no sunlight, particularly during winter**. So, point IV is correct.**
**Knowledge Box** **Difference between Sulphurous smog and Photochemical Smog:**
● **Sulphurous smog is** also known as **industrial smog, classical smog, or London smog** **while Photochemical smog** is also called Los **Angels Smog.**
● forms in the presence of **sunlight** from reactions between NOₓ and VOCs, while **sulphurous smog** forms in **cold, foggy conditions** due to the presence of **sulphur dioxide** **and particulates**.
● Photochemical smog primarily consists of **ground-level ozone** and **particulate matter**. The ozone in this type of smog is formed through a chemical reaction between **volatile** **organic compounds (VOCs)** and **nitrogen oxides (NOₓ)** in the presence of sunlight.
● VOCs are carbon-based compounds that readily evaporate into the atmosphere and originate from sources such as **paints, pesticides, and household products**.
● They are also emitted through **fuel combustion**, particularly from **motor vehicles** and even some **lawn equipment**.
● While sulphurous smog was more widespread in the past, photochemical smog is now the more common type. Both the smog comprises toxic pollutants and are the reason for high mortality in areas, where such smog is a common feature.
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
If a substance is said to have a negative radiative
forcing, this implies that:

options.a: The substance has a net cooling effect on Earth
options.b: The substance increases the humidity of a place
options.c: The substance has a net heating effect on Earth
options.d:
The substance decreases the humidity of the
place

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Radiative forcing** or climate forcing happens when the Earth receives more or less energy from the Sun than it sends back into space. Energy comes in as sunlight and leaves as heat.
● If more **energy comes in than goes out, the Earth gets warmer.** This imbalance in energy is what we call radiative forcing, and it can lead to changes in the Earth’s climate.
● Many factors, such as **clouds**, **polar ice**, and the **properties of gases** in the atmosphere, affect this energy balance by either **reflecting sunlight**, **trapping heat**, or **absorbing radiation**, thereby influencing how much energy stays in or leaves the Earth’s system.
● Radioactive forcing can be positive or negative depending on the amount of solar energy that enters or leaves the earth.
o **Positive radiative forcing** occurs when **more solar energy enters** the Earth’s system than leaves it, causing the **Earth’s atmosphere to warm**. o **Negative radiative forcing** happens when **more energy leaves** the Earth’s system than comes in, leading to a **cooling effect** on the atmosphere.
● **Radiative forcing** is a useful predictor of globally-averaged temperature change due to the natural or human-induced changes to the energy budget.
● Increases in greenhouse gas concentrations over the industrial era are responsible for a positive climate forcing, causing a gain of energy in the climate system
● In contrast, changes in atmospheric aerosol concentrations result in a negative climate forcing leading to a loss of energy.
● According to the latest Global averages 2018, **Total radiative forcing** is mainly driven by the **positive** **forcing from carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, which warms the Earth's atmosphere. Other gases like **methane** **(CH₄)** and **ozone (O₃)** also add to this warming. However, this effect is **partly balanced** by the **negative** **radiative forcing of aerosols**, which have a cooling influence. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Soil Pollution, Solid Waste, Land Degradation
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Soils derived from igneous rocks are more vulnerable to acidification.
**Statement II:** Unlike sedimentary rocks, igneous rocks lack alkaline components.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct,
but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● **Acid rain** encompasses various forms of precipitation and atmospheric deposits containing acidic components. It primarily results from the **reaction of sulphur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides** **(NOx)** with atmospheric moisture, forming sulfuric and nitric acids, which then fall to the earth as wet deposition (rain, snow, etc.) or dry deposition (Tiny acidic particles and aerosols can be carried by the wind and settle on surfaces).
● Acid rain causes **rock acidification** by chemically reacting with minerals, especially in **calcium** **carbonate-rich rocks** like **limestone** and **marble**, leading to their **dissolution or structural alteration**. This can result in nutrient leaching, toxic metal mobilization and nutrient inaccessibility. The **impact of** **acid rain on rock weathering** depends on the **acidity (pH)** of rainwater and the **composition of the** **underlying rock**.
● **Igneous rocks** are generally **more resistant to weathering** than sedimentary rocks due to their **dense,** **interlocking crystalline structure** formed from the solidification of magma or lava. This compact structure limits the penetration of water and other weathering agents.   In contrast, **sedimentary rocks** are composed of **loosely bound fragments or chemical precipitates**, making them **more susceptible to** **mechanical and chemical weathering**, such as dissolution and erosion.
● However, when acid rain falls on igneous rocks, it is more vulnerable to acidification than the sedimentary rock. When acid rain falls on limestone-rich (sedimentary) rocks, such as those found in Rajasthan, the alkaline minerals (like calcium carbonate), the rock neutralizes the acid. This reaction also slowly dissolves the limestone. In contrast, **igneous rocks (like granite, quartz sandstone, or shale), which are** **mainly composed of silica (SiO₂) and alumina (Al₂O₃), lack such alkaline components**. They provide low buffering capacity, making **soils derived from igneous rocks more vulnerable to acidification**. **So,** **statement I and statement II are correct.**
● Although **igneous rocks are more resistant to weathering**, their **poor acid-neutralizing ability** leads to **ecosystem acidification** when exposed to acid rain over time. **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to
Particulate Matter (PM):
I. - It includes particles like dust, soot, and pollen.
II. 	 All PMs are classified as short-lived climate
pollutants.
III. 	 The shorter the diameter of the PM the deeper it
travels into the lungs.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Particulate Matter** is made up of a mixture of **microscopic solids and liquid droplets** suspended in air. It includes **acids** (such as nitrates and sulphates), organic chemicals, metals, soil or dust particles, and allergens (such as fragments of pollen or mold spores). These particles come in many sizes and shapes and can be made up of hundreds of different chemicals. These particles are so small that they can be inhaled and cause serious health problems. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Particle size is directly linked to the potential** for causing health problems. PM10 is particulate matter 10 micrometers or less in diameter, PM2.5 is particulate matter 2.5 micrometers or less in diameter.
o PM10 and are commonly present in air that may be drawn into the body with every breath. **The** **shorter the diameter of the PM the deeper it travels into the lungs**. PM 2.5 is the smallest and most harmful. In contrast, larger particles mainly cause irritation to the eyes, nose, and throat but pose less serious health risks. **PM****2.5** **is generally described as fine particles** (found in smoke and haze). **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)** are highly potent climate forcers that stay in the atmosphere for a much shorter time than carbon dioxide but have a significantly greater warming potential.
● The main SLCPs include **black carbon (BC), a substantial part of particulate matter (PM2.5),** methane (CH4) and ground-level ozone (O3) and **hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)**. They are the main drivers of human-induced global warming after CO₂, contributing to **up to 45%** of current global warming. Not all particulate matter is short lived. Particles like dust, soot, and certain aerosols from sources like construction, agriculture, industrial processes, and wildfires remain in the atmosphere for a longer period of time. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Marine dead zones are increasing because of global warming.
**Statement II:** The warmer the surface waters, the higher the capacity of oceans to hold carbon dioxide. **Statement III:** Melting of glaciers creates a strong density difference between the lighter freshwater at the top and heavier saltwater below.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are correct
and both of them explain Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are correct,
but only one of them explains Statement–I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements–II and III is correct
and that explains Statement–I
options.d:
Neither Statement–II nor Statement–III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Dead zones form when oxygen levels** in seawater fall too low to sustain marine life, a condition known as hypoxia. This is **mainly caused by nutrient pollution,** particularly nitrogen and phosphorus, which enter the ocean from sources like agricultural fertilizers, sewage, and industrial runoff. These excess nutrients fuel massive algal blooms that can cover vast areas of coastal and open ocean waters.
● **When the algae die**, they are decomposed by bacteria—a process that consumes large amounts of oxygen. Due to the overwhelming quantity of decomposing algae, oxygen is used up more quickly than it can be replaced, leading to **hypoxic conditions** that can suffocate marine organisms.
● **Climate change** feeds this process as it intensifies rainfall, causing more nutrient runoff into rivers and coasts. Climate change is **worsening both the spread and severity of ocean dead zones.** As ocean temperatures rise faster than ever recorded, warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen, contributing directly to hypoxic conditions. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Climate change** also impacts **carbon sequestration by the oceans**. Once surface water becomes saturated because of more absorption, the ocean's ability to absorb carbon would slow down.
● **Warmer temperatures reduce the ocean's capacity to hold CO₂,** causing it to release some back into the air—similar to a warm, flat soda. To continue absorbing carbon, **the ocean relies on upwelling, where** **deep waters rich in dissolved carbonate rise to the surface,** allowing new surface water to take in more CO₂ while the old water sinks with the carbon it collected.
● The **warmer the surface water becomes**, the harder it is for winds to mix the surface layers with the deeper layers. The ocean settles into layers or stratifies. Without an infusion of fresh carbonate-rich water from below, **the surface water saturates with carbon dioxide**. The stagnant water also supports fewer phytoplankton, and carbon dioxide uptake from photosynthesis slows. In short, stratification cuts down the amount of carbon the ocean can take up. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● This **limits the supply of carbonate for buffering acidification and reduces phytoplankton growth** (which normally absorb CO₂ and release oxygen). Without this mixing, deeper waters become oxygendepleted, contributing to the dead zone. The run off also increases nutrient runoff and algal bloom that depletes oxygen. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, only one of the Statements–II and III is correct and that explains Statement–I** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Ocean acidification increases the mottling of calcified structures of marine organisms.
**Statement II:** Hydrogen ions have a higher affinity for carbonate ions than calcium ions.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct,
but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A significant amount of the CO2** released into the atmosphere by human activities, like burning fossil fuels, is **absorbed by the ocean**. When water (H2O) and CO2 mix, they combine to form carbonic acid (H2CO3). It releases hydrogen ions (H+), which bond with other molecules in the area lowering the ocean’s pH.
● One of the molecules that **hydrogen ions bond with is carbonate (CO****3****-2****),** a key component of calcium carbonate (CaCO3) shells. This makes **carbonate less abundant** ions that corals, oysters, mussels, and many other shelled organisms need to build shells and skeletons. It also **increases the mottling of** **calcified structures** of marine organisms. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Marine animals like **corals and oysters** build their shells by combining **calcium ions (Ca²⁺)** with **carbonate ions (CO₃²⁻)** from seawater. This forms **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** and releases **carbon** **dioxide and water**. However, this shell-building process is **very sensitive to acidity**. In acidic water, **hydrogen ions (H⁺)** compete with calcium ions to bond with carbonate.
● Hydrogen ions have a **stronger attraction** to carbonate, so they often form **bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)** instead. The problem is that **shell-building animals can't use bicarbonate to make shells**, so they struggle to get the carbonate they need for growth. If there are too many hydrogen ions around and not enough molecules for them to bond with, they **can even begin breaking existing calcium carbonate** molecules apart— dissolving shells that already exist. **So, statement II is correct.**
**Knowledge Box** **pH Value:**
● An **acid is a substance that releases H****+** **ions**; and **pH is the scale** used to measure the concentration of H+ ions.
● Seawater that has **more hydrogen ions is more acidic** by definition, and it also has a lower pH.
● The **lower the pH, the more acidic the solution** and more H+ ions.
● The pH scale goes from **extremely basic at 14 to extremely acidic** at 0 and with a pH of 7 being neutral (neither acidic or basic).
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are secondary pollutants?
I. 	 Sulphuric acid
II. 	 Peroxyacyl nitrates
III. 	Ozone
IV. 	 Nitric acid
V. 	 Carbon monoxide
Select the correct answer using the code below:

options.b: II, III, IV and V only
options.d: I, II, III and IV only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pollutants** are classified as primary or secondary. **Primary pollutants are directly released into the air** from both natural and human-made (anthropogenic) sources, while **secondary pollutants form in the** **atmosphere through chemical reactions.** These pollutants can originate locally but may also be transported or formed over long distances. They impact ecosystems, climate, and human health at both local and global scales.
● **Primary pollutants:** o Primary pollutants are substances emitted directly into the atmosphere from a specific source, such as factories, vehicles, or natural events like volcanic eruptions. Examples include **carbon**
**monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2),** nitrogen oxides (NOx), and particulate matter (like soot and ash). **So, points I and V are not correct.**
● **Secondary pollutants:** o They are formed when **primary pollutants react with other elements** in the atmosphere, such as oxygen, water vapor, and hydrocarbons. o These reactions can **create harmful compounds** that weren't originally present. o A key example is **photochemical smog,** which forms through the interaction of sunlight with primary pollutants. o This results in yellowish clouds made up of secondary pollutants like **ozone** (O₃), **peroxyacyl** **nitrates (PANs), and nitric acid**, all of which are harmful to human health. **So, points II, III and** **IV are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Stratospheric Aerosol Injection aims to reduce the insolation over the Earth's surface.
**Statement II:** Greenhouse gases like carbon dioxide entraps short wave solar radiation from the Earth's atmosphere.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are
correct, but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Geoengineering, or climate engineering**, refers to large-scale interventions in the Earth's natural systems such as the oceans, land, and atmosphere to directly address climate change. These methods generally fall into two main categories:
o Techniques aimed at **removing carbon dioxide (CO₂)** from the atmosphere, and o Approaches that seek to **reduce the amount of sunlight** reaching the Earth's surface to cool the planet.
● **Stratospheric Aerosol Injection** (SAI) is one such man-made (artificial) method where tiny particles called sulfate aerosols are released into the stratosphere where m**ost of the ozone is found. This layer** absorbs harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation and heats the upper atmosphere. **Stratospheric Aerosol** **Injection** **technique mimics volcanic eruptions**, which naturally release particles into the atmosphere that can **reflect some of the Sun’s rays back into space**, cooling the Earth's surface for a short time. SAI tries to copy this cooling **effect to help reduce global warming** not by cutting greenhouse gas emissions directly, but by offsetting their heating effect. **So, Statement I is correct.**
● **As sunlight enters the atmosphere, it gets scattered, reflected, or absorbed by air, clouds, and** **particles. The green-house gases** primarily trap infrared radiation (heat) emitted from the Earth's surface**,** **not sunlight directly.** Sunlight passes through the atmosphere and warms the Earth's surface. This warmed surface then emits infrared radiation (long wave) which is then absorbed by greenhouse gases, leading to a warming effect. **So, Statement II is not correct.** **So, Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is incorrect** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **Sun’s surface temperature is around 5,480°C**, and it emits radiant energy that travels through space and reaches Earth’s atmosphere.
● This solar energy consists **mainly of visible light,** shortwave radiation (like ultraviolet), and longwave radiation (like infrared). While **some of this energy is reflected back into** **space** by the outer atmosphere, the rest passes through to Earth.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Suburban regions often report higher concentrations of ground-level ozone.
**Statement II:** Suburban regions often receive uninterrupted sunlight. **Statement III:** Suburban regions have reduced presence of nitrogen oxides.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● At the **ground level, ozone (O₃)** is not emitted directly from any source. Instead, it is a secondary pollutant formed through complex photochemical reactions.
● Ground-level ozone (O₃) is a **secondary pollutant** formed when **VOCs and NOₓ**, emitted from **vehicles,** **industries, and vegetation**, react under **strong sunlight**, particularly on hot summer days.
● However, the distribution of ground-level ozone does not always align with the areas of highest pollution emissions. In these highly polluted urban cores, **excessive amounts of nitrogen oxides (especially nitric** **oxide, or NO) can actually suppress ozone formation** through a process called **NO titration.** In this reaction, **nitric oxide reacts with ozone and breaks it down**, thus reducing ozone concentrations locally despite the high availability of its precursors.
● In contrast, **greener or suburban areas**, especially those located **downwind of urban centers**, often report **higher concentrations of ground-level ozone**. **So, Statement I is correct.**
● This pattern emerges due to several interacting factors: o **Lower NOₓ levels**: In suburban or rural settings, the reduced presence of nitrogen oxides means there is **less ozone destruction** via NO titration. This allows more ozone to remain in the atmosphere. **So, Statement III is correct.** o **Presence of biogenic VOCs**: These areas typically have **abundant vegetation**, which naturally emits **biogenic volatile organic compounds** such as isoprene and monoterpenes. These compounds, though natural, serve as **active precursors** in the photochemical formation of ozone when combined with NOₓ and sunlight. o **Strong sunlight and stagnant air**: These regions often receive **uninterrupted sunlight** and may experience **poor atmospheric mixing**, which enhances the photochemical reactions and prevents the dispersion of pollutants. This creates favorable conditions for **ozone buildup** during the afternoon and early evening hours. **So, Statement II is correct.**
● **Ozone (O₃)**, regardless of where it is found (stratosphere or troposphere), is **inherently a reactive and** **unstable molecule** because it readily gives up one of its oxygen atoms (O) to oxidize other substances.
● A significant portion of **the incoming shortwave UV radiation is absorbed by oxygen** **(O₂) and ozone (O₃) in the upper atmosphere.**
● On the other hand, the Earth emits longwave (infrared) radiation back into space, but greenhouse gases such as water vapor (H₂O), carbon dioxide (CO₂), methane (CH₄), nitrous oxide (N₂O), and tropospheric ozone (O₃) absorb much of this outgoing heat—trapping it in the atmosphere and contributing to the greenhouse effect.
● At the **ground level**, this reactivity makes it a **strong oxidant**, capable of damaging **living tissues**, **materials**, and contributing to **air pollution.** So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Ground-level ozone breached safe limits in Delhi and ozone was the lead pollutant

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to
Volatile Organic Compounds:
I. - These compounds evaporate easily into the air due to their high vapour pressure.
II. 	 These mainly include any compound of carbon
like carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Volatile Organic Compound (VOCs) are organic chemicals** having high vapour pressure at room temperature.  They **are organic chemical compounds whose composition makes it possible for them** **to evaporate under normal** indoor atmospheric conditions of temperature and pressure. It includes any **compound of carbon except carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, carbonic acid, metallic carbides or** **carbonates** and ammonium carbonate that participate in atmospheric photochemistry. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● **Characteristics of Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs):** o VOCs easily **evaporate into the air due to their high vapour pressure** (typically > 0.1 mmHg at 25°C). This makes them easily airborne even at normal environmental temperatures. **So,** **Statement I is correct.** o VOCs **usually have low boiling points (below 250°C at standard pressure).** E.g Formaldehyde: ~−19°C, Benzene: ~80°C.
● **VOCs are universal atmospheric species** emitted by both natural and anthropogenic sources. On the basis of emission source, VOCs can be categorized into two types: **Anthropogenic VOCs and Biogenic** **VOCs**.
o **Anthropogenic VOCs:** These are emitted from combustion sources such as emissions from vehicle and fossil-fuelled power plants, fuel storage and transport, solvent usage etc. o **Biogenic sources of VOCs:** These are mainly emitted from vegetation and natural processes including bushfires, lightning and the microbial processes that occur in soil generate nitrogen oxides etc.
● **VOCs are harmful pollutants** that cause both acute and chronic health issues. They play a key role in forming **ground-level ozone and photochemical smog** by reacting with NOx in sunlight, especially in urban areas.
● VOCs also **contribute to PM2.5** through secondary organic aerosols and include toxic compounds like polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), known for their carcinogenic and mutagenic effects. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText: The Kyoto Protocol was associated with:

options.a:
United Nations Convention on Biological
Diversity
options.b:
United Nations Framework Convention on
Climate  Change
options.c:
United Nations Convention to Combat
Desertification

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Kyoto Protocol (1997) operationalized the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change by **committing industrialized countries and economies in transition to limit and reduce** **greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions** in accordance with agreed individual targets.
● The Kyoto Protocol is based on the principles and provisions of the Convention and follows its annexbased structure. It **only binds developed countries, and places a heavier burden on them** under the principle of “common but differentiated responsibility and respective capabilities”. It recognizes that they are largely responsible for the current high levels of GHG emissions in the atmosphere. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements justifies the
significance of Article 6 of the Paris Agreement?
I. - It enables the exchange of carbon credits among countries to help them achieve their emission reduction targets.
II. 	 It recognizes both market-based and non-
market-based approaches to address climate change mitigation.
III. 	 It adopts a 'corresponding adjustment' mechanism to avoid the double-counting of carbon credits.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 6 of the Paris Agreement** allows countries to **voluntarily cooperate with each** other to achieve emission reduction targets set out in their Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs).
● Under Article 6, a country (or countries) will be able to **transfer carbon credits earned from the** **reduction of GHG emissions** to help one or more countries meet climate targets. Within Article 6,  Article
6.2 creates the basis for trading in GHG emission reductions (or “mitigation outcomes”) across countries. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Article 6.2 allows countries to trade emission reductions and removals with one another through bilateral or multilateral agreements. These traded credits are called Internationally Transferred Mitigation Outcomes (ITMOs). ○ Article 6.4 will create a global carbon market overseen by a United Nations entity, referred to as the “Article 6.4 Supervisory Body” (6.4SB). Project developers will request to register their projects with the Supervisory Body. A project must be approved by both the country where it is implemented, and the Supervisory Body, before it can start issuing UN-recognised credits. These credits, known as A6.4ERs, can be bought by countries, companies, or even individuals.
● Article 6 of the Paris Agreement establishes guidelines for cooperative approaches to reduce emissions and fulfil Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs). These cooperative approaches encompass both **carbon markets (as described in Articles 6.2 and 6.4) and non-market strategies (as described in** **Article 6.8)**. Article 6.8 **recognizes non-market approaches to promote mitigation and adaptation**. It introduces cooperation through finance, technology transfer, and capacity building, where no trading of emission reductions is involved. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Under Article 6, emission reductions that have been authorized for transfer by the selling country’s government may be sold to another country, but only one country may count the emission reduction toward its NDC. It is critical to **avoid double counting** so that global emission reductions are not overestimated. The agreement on Article 6 **established an accounting mechanism known as** **“corresponding adjustment,” to ensure that double counting does not occur. So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Pollution Related (E-Waste, Solid Waste, Pollutants, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the
‘Stockholm Convention’:
I. - It is a public-private treaty under the aegis of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change.
II. 	 It classifies pollutants into three Annexes,
depending on the restrictions to be imposed on them.
III. 	 The Green Climate Fund acts as the primary
funding agency for the Convention.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants** is a global treaty to protect human health and the environment from certain chemicals called Persistent Organic Pollutants. These are chemicals that remain intact in the environment for long periods, become widely distributed geographically, accumulate in the fatty tissue of humans and wildlife, and have harmful impacts on human health and the environment.
● The Stockholm Convention was **adopted and opened for signature in May 2001** in Stockholm, Sweden. The Convention entered into force on 17 May 2004.
○ The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC ) is a separate treaty that was open to signature in 1992 at the Rio Earth Summit and entered into force on 21 March 1994. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Stockholm Convention **lists chemicals in three Annexes:** Annex A lists chemicals to be eliminated; Annex B lists chemicals to be restricted; and Annex C calls for minimizing unintentional production and release of listed chemicals. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Global Environment Facility serves as a "financial mechanism"** to the Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants. Article 14 of the Stockholm Convention entrusts the GEF as its financial mechanism. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Initiative Launched Under
Primary Objective
I. - Great Green Wall Initiative
Convention on Migratory Species
Restoration of degraded landscapes
II. - Peace Forest Initiative
United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification
Restoration of degraded forests in conflictaffected locations
III. - Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety
Convention on Biological Diversity
Equitable sharing of benefits arising from genetic resources
IV. - Lima Work Programme on Gender
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change
Gender balance and women empowerment
In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Great Green Wall Initiative (GGWI)** is a flagship initiative of the African Union aiming to **restore the** **continent’s degraded landscapes (restore 100 million hectares of land, sequester 250 million tonnes** **of carbon and create 10 million jobs)** and transform millions of lives by tackling the triple challenges of desertification, climate change and loss of biodiversity. It was **launched by the African Union in 2007.** **So, row I is not correctly matched.**
● **The Peace Forest Initiative (PFI) is a flagship programme of the United Nations Convention to** **Combat Desertification (UNCCD)** that demonstrates crucial linkages between land, peace and security. It is designed to address **restoration of ecosystems and land-based resources including land, soil,** **water and forests in fragile and conflict-affected locations.** **So, row II is correctly matched.**
○ The PFI brings together stakeholders to catalyse transboundary cooperation on ecosystem restoration to build trust and peace. It aims to **rehabilitate and restore degraded land, soil, water** **and forests.**
● **The Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety (CPB)** was adopted in 2000 as **a supplementary agreement to** **the Convention on Biological Diversity** and entered into force in 2003.
○ The objective of the Protocol is to contribute to **ensuring an adequate level of protection in the** **field of the safe transfer, handling and use of living modified organisms (LMOs)** resulting from modern biotechnology that may have adverse effects on the conservation and sustainable use of biological diversity. ○ **The Nagoya Protocol** to the Convention on Biological Diversity is an international agreement
which **aims at sharing the benefits arising from the utilization of genetic resources in a fair** **and equitable way. So, information in row III is not correctly matched.**
● **The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) adopted** the enhanced **Lima Work Programme on Gender (LWPG)** and Gender Action Plan (GAP) in 2019. The LWPG aims
to **ensure gender balance in decision-making and empower women in climate policy implementation.** **It aims to achieve gender responsive climate policy and action. So, information in row IV is correctly** **matched.** **So, in only two of the above rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD):
I. 	 It is the only legally binding international
agreement that addresses desertification and the effects of drought.
II. 	 It was an outcome of the Rio Earth Summit of
1992.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD) was established in 1994 to protect and restore our land and ensure a safer, just, and more sustainable future.
● The United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD) is the **only legally binding** **international agreement linking environment and development to sustainable land management.** It is the driving force behind Sustainable Development Goal 15 and Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN). **So, statement I is correct.**
○ The Convention addresses specifically the arid, semi-arid, and dry sub-humid areas, known as the drylands, where some of the most vulnerable ecosystems and peoples can be found.
● **The 1992 Rio Earth Summit** gave rise to the three Rio Conventions: the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), and the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD). **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Greenhouse Gases (GHG), ODS, Related Terms, Global Warming
questions.questionText:
Refrigerants like chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)
and hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) act as greenhouse gases with high global warming potential. Even though they significantly contribute to climate change, which one of the following reasons make it feasible for their persistent use?

options.a:
Their presence in atmosphere is low in
quantity
options.b: These refrigerants have low boiling points
options.c:
They are cheaply produced compared to their
alternatives

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A refrigerant is a chemical that has special properties that allow it to absorb or release heat easily under different conditions. They are used to control temperatures or cool areas. Global warming potential is a measure of how much a given mass of a chemical substance contributes to global warming over a specific period.
● Many substances used as refrigerants exhibit high GWP values, acting as powerful greenhouse gases capable of trapping more heat in the atmosphere than CO₂. The main issue with many refrigerants is that **they significantly contribute to climate change. Refrigerants like** chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) **act as greenhouse gases with high global warming potential.**
● In the refrigeration cycle, work is done on the refrigerant to either condense it to a liquid or evaporate it to a gas so that it can quickly adjust the temperature of the spaces being refrigerated. To do so, **these** **refrigerants must have low boiling points** and the ability to change phases without a lot of work. Because they need these special properties, only a certain variety of chemicals can be properly used. As a result, **there are three main types of refrigerants that have been consistently used over time:** **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs), and Hydrofluorocarbons** **(HFCs)**. All three of these categories of refrigerants hold the important properties required to efficiently cool areas. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Global Cooling Pledge,
consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** India is yet to officially join the Global Cooling Pledge.
**Statement II:** Global Cooling Pledge demands binding commitment from the countries to achieve emission reduction targets. **Statements III:** Fulfilling the Global Cooling Pledge can impact the access to affordable cooling for people in India.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the statements given above?

options.a:
Both statement II and statement III are correct
and both of them explain statement I
options.b:
Both statement II and statement III are correct,
but only one of them explains statement I
options.c:
Only one of the statements II and III is correct,
and that explains statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Global Cooling Pledge is an initiative of the United Arab Emirates and UNEP.** The Pledge is one of nine non-negotiated declarations, pledges, and charters that constitute key outcomes for the COP28 Presidential Action Agenda.
● The Global Cooling Pledge provides an opportunity to commit to sustainable cooling with concrete actions. It aims to **reduce cooling related emissions by 68% from today by 2050,** significantly increase access to sustainable cooling by 2030, and increase the global average efficiency of new air conditioners by 50%.
● **The Global Cooling Pledge is non-binding.** The Pledge is non-binding and individual countries are not assigned targets. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **India is yet to sign this global pledge due to the need for the world's most populous country to have** **affordable cooling.** The pledge would require major investments by countries to shift to sustainable cooling technologies and also raise the cost of such products.
○ In 2024, parts of India experienced temperatures over 50°C, underscoring the need for cooling to ensure thermal comfort, preserve food, medical supplies, and industrial processes. India, one of the most climate-vulnerable nations, faces increasingly severe heatwaves that endanger millions. ○ Thus, cooling demand in India is expected to surge, with air conditioner sales rising 16% for every degree over 30°C. ○ By 2050, India may have the world’s highest cooling demand, with over 1.14 billion air conditioners in use. **So, statement III is correct and that explains statement -I.** **So, only one of the statements II and III is correct, and that explains statement I** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**India Cooling Action Plan:**
● India is one of the first countries in the world to develop a comprehensive Cooling Action plan. The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has released the India Cooling Action Plan in March, 2019.
● The India Cooling Action Plan (ICAP) provides an integrated vision towards cooling across sectors. The overarching goal of ICAP is to provide sustainable cooling and thermal comfort for all while securing environmental and socio-economic benefits for the society.
● The India Cooling Action Plan seeks to ○ Reduce cooling demand across sectors by 20% to 25% by 2037-38 ○ Reduce refrigerant demand by 25% to 30% by 2037-38 ○ Reduce cooling energy requirements by 25% to 40% by 2037-38 ○ Recognize "cooling and related areas" as a thrust area of research under national S&T Programme ○ Training and certification of 100,000 servicing sector technicians by 2022-23, synergizing with Skill India Mission

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Powering Past Coal Alliance
II. 	 Hydrogen Declaration
III. 	Kigali Agreement
IV. 	 Glasgow Leaders' Declaration on Forests and
Land Use
V. 	 Global Renewables and Energy Efficiency
Pledge India is **not** presently a member/signatory of how many of the above initiatives?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Powering Past Coal Alliance (PPCA)** is a coalition of national and subnational governments, businesses and organisations working to advance the transition from unabated coal power generation to clean energy. It was launched at COP23 of UNFCCC in 2017. India **has not yet joined the Alliance.** **India is committed to meeting the energy needs of its people and will also have to rely on coal power.** **So, point I is correct.**
● **COP29 Hydrogen Declaration** aims to catalyse a global clean hydrogen market. It was adopted at COP 29 of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change. It aims to accelerate the production and use of clean hydrogen to decarbonize various sectors and achieve climate goals. It is **a non-legally** **binding document** and does not require any changes to be made to the participating countries' national law. **India is not a signatory to the Declaration. So, point II is correct.**
● **The Kigali Amendment** to the Montreal Protocol aims for the phase-down of hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) by cutting their production and consumption. The goal is to achieve over 80% reduction in HFC consumption by 2047. Kigali Agreement is an amendment to the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer. The Kigali Amendment is a legally binding agreement, which came into force from January 1, 2019.  In September 2021, **India officially ratified the Kigali Amendment of the** **Montreal Protocol.** **So, point III is not correct.**
● **The Glasgow Leaders' Declaration on Forests and Land Use (GDFLU)** was launched at the November 2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference. It aims to ‘halt and reverse forest loss and land degradation by 2030 while delivering sustainable development and promoting inclusive rural transformation.’ **India is among the 10 most forest-rich countries in the world. But it did not sign this** **declaration. So, point IV is correct.**
○ India apparently did not sign as it was not happy with the effort to link infrastructure development and related activities with the conservation of forests in the prepared text.
● **The Global Renewables and Energy Efficiency Pledge**, signed at COP28, commits to tripling worldwide installed renewable energy generation capacity to at least 11,000 GW and to double the global average annual rate of energy efficiency improvements to more than 4% by 2030. It also calls for a phasedown of 'unabated coal power’. **India, along with China, abstained from signing the Global** **Renewables and Energy Efficiency Pledge.**  India has objected to specific focus on coal. **So, option V** **is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
With reference to the significance of using ethanol-
blended petrol in vehicles, consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Ethanol-blended petrol generally leads to reductions in harmful exhaust emissions.
**Statement II:** Ethanol acts as an oxygenate that helps in better fuel combustion. **Statement III:** Ethanol has a higher energy density than petrol.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the statements given above?

options.a:
Both statement II and statement III are correct
and both of them explain statement I
options.b:
Both statement II and statement III are correct,
but only one of them explains statement I
options.c:
Only one of the statements II and III is correct,
and that explains statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Ethanol, being a biofuel, replaces fossil-based petrol and significantly cuts down CO2 emissions. A NITI Aayog study found that lifecycle emissions from sugarcane-based ethanol are 65% lower than petrol, while maize-based ethanol emits 50% less greenhouse gases.
● Ethanol (CHOH) burns more cleanly than petrol. **It acts as an oxygenate, helping fuel combust more** **completely and reducing harmful emissions** like carbon monoxide and unburned hydrocarbons. It also works as a fuel extender, reducing reliance on non-renewable petroleum. **Using ethanol-blended petrol** **generally leads to reductions in harmful exhaust emissions**, particularly carbon monoxide (CO), hydrocarbons (HC), and particulate matter (PM). This is due to ethanol’s oxygen content, which helps achieve cleaner combustion. **So, statement II is correct and explains statement I.**
● Ethanol has slightly lower energy content than petrol. **Ethanol has a lower energy density than petrol.** **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ As the ethanol percentage in the blend increases, engines tend to experience a slight decrease in fuel economy, meaning a bit more fuel is needed for the same driving distance. **So, only one of the statements II and III is correct, and that explains statement I** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas has issued a detailed clarification dismissing the fears regarding concerns over the impact of 20% ethanol-blended petrol (E20) on vehicle performance.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the
‘New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG)’, which was recently seen in the news:
I. - It is part of the Paris Agreement.
II. 	 It sets emission reduction targets that all
countries must achieve by 2035.
III. 	 India has rejected it because it doesn't reflect the
priorities of the Global South.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **New Collective Quantified Goal on Climate Finance (NCQG)** was agreed at COP 29 i.e. 29th meeting of the Conference of the Parties (COP) to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC).
● NCQG is basically **a target for finance.** It **indicates the funds that should be mobilised annually** to support climate action projects in the developing countries. The NCQG is the new amount that must be mobilised by developed countries every year from 2025 onward to finance climate action in developing countries**. So, statement II is not correct.**
● **NCQG is a key element of the Paris Agreement. Under the** New Collective Quantified Goal**,** governments agreed to set a new climate finance target by 2025 that would channel money into these nations and help them tackle climate change.
○ Triple finance to developing countries, from the previous goal of USD 100 billion annually, to USD 300 billion annually by 2035. ○ Secure efforts of all actors to work together to scale up finance to developing countries, from public and private sources, to the amount of USD 1.3 trillion per year by 2035. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **India was the first to reject the decision** because it doesn't reflect the priorities of the Global South. The rejection has since been endorsed by several developing countries. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** New Collective Quantified Goal on Climate Finance (NCQG) agreed at COP 29.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
