● Founded in 2012, and convened within **UNEP,** The Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) is a **voluntary partnership** of more than 190 governments, intergovernmental organizations, and non-governmental organizations.
● The CCAC works to reduce powerful but short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs) that drive both climate change and air pollution.
● It aims to connect ambitious agenda setting with targeted mitigation action within countries and sectors. Robust science and analysis underpin its efforts. It supports action to reduce short-lived climate pollutants in more than 70 countries through the funding of projects and the individual actions of its partners.
● The partnership works to **reduce global warming** in the near-term to achieve Paris Agreement goals and support economic development, improved health, and environmental and food security benefits.
● The CCAC funds projects that implement methane policy and mitigation measures around the world. It also provides secretariat functions of the **Global Methane Pledge** and the coordination group of Lowering Organic Waste Methane Initiative is housed in CCAC. **Relevance**: At COP29 climate summit, experts urged India to prioritize reducing short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs) like methane to combat its severe air pollution and global warming.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Presence of pine trees
II. 	 Transhumance activities
III. 	Shifting cultivation
Which of the above are among the primary reasons for forest fire in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Forest fires, also known as wildfires or bushfires**, are **uncontrolled fires** occurring in areas densely populated with flammable vegetation, characterized by their large size, rapid propagation, and potential to change direction unexpectedly.
● They can be ignited by **natural causes or human negligence** and pose significant hazards, including loss of life and environmental damage.
● Forest fires are caused by natural causes as well as man-made causes.
○ **Natural causes**- Many forest fires start from natural causes such as lightning which set trees on fire. However, rain extinguishes such fires without causing much damage. High atmospheric temperatures and dryness (low humidity) offer favourable circumstances for a fire to start. ○ **Man-made causes**- Fire is caused when a source of fire like naked flame, cigarette or bidi, electric spark or any source of ignition comes into contact with inflammable material.
● Although there are multiple other reasons for occurrence of human induced forest fires, forest fires in the country can be segregated in four prominent clusters:
● Fires in the **Western Himalayan region** (Jammu & Kashmir, Uttarakhand, and Himachal Pradesh) are caused mainly on account of fires in pine forests. **Pine forests are vulnerable to fires** due to their flammable pine needles, high resin content, and the accumulation of dry needles on the forest floor that create fuel. **So, point I is correct.**
○ There is no pattern to link fire in mountains with transhumance activity, apart from occasional fires. **So, point II is not correct.**
● Fires in **western part of the country** are caused mainly on account of drier vegetation and extensive grassland habitats.
● Fires in **Central Indian states, Western and Eastern Ghats** are caused mainly on account of large scale collection of Non-Timber Forest Products (NTFPs). **So, point III is correct.**
○ During NTFP collection, branches are often lopped for harvesting fruits, fires are lit at the base of trees to stimulate resin flow and the bark is collected in a destructive manner. High extraction rates of some NTFPs resulted in poor density, sparse distribution of species and depletion of biodiversity.
● Wide spread fires in **North-Eastern states** are caused mainly due to **shifting cultivation**. **So, pair III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: According to the Forest Survey of India (FSI) data, Odisha recorded about 27,120 forest fire incidents between Nov 2024 and June 2025.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB):
I. 	 It is a statutory body set up under the
provisions of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
II. 	 It is responsible for publishing for the
National Air Quality Index for Indian cities.
III. 	It serves as the appellate body for appeals
against the orders of the State Pollution Control Boards.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, is a **statutory organisation** constituted in September, 1974 under **the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**. Further, CPCB was entrusted with the powers and functions under **the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● The CPCB functions under the administrative control of the **Ministry of Environment and Forests and** **Climate Change (MoEFCC),** Government of India.
● The CPCB serves as a **field formation** and also provides **technical services** to the MoEFCC of the provisions of **the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.**
● **Air Quality Monitoring** is an important part of air quality management.
● The **National Air Monitoring Programme** **(NAMP)** has been established with objectives to determine the present air quality status and trends and to control and regulate pollution from industries and other sources to meet the air quality standards. It also provides background air quality data needed for industrial siting and town planning.
● The **National Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a system that transforms complex air pollution data into a single, easily understandable number, color, and description to inform the public about air quality and associated health risks.
● There are **six AQI categories**, namely Good, Satisfactory, Moderately polluted, Poor, Very Poor, and Severe.  The AQI considers **eight pollutants** (PM10, PM2.5, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, and Pb) for which
short-term (up to 24-hourly averaging period) National Ambient Air Quality Standards are prescribed. The AQI in India is published by the **CPCB. So, statement II is correct.**
● The **National Green Tribunal,** setup under the **National Green Tribunal Act 2010** for effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests and other natural resources including enforcement of any legal right relating to environment and giving relief and compensation for damages to persons and property and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto.
● **The NGT serves as the appellate body for dispute resolution in cases involving pollution control** **boards and other environmental regulators in India**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: According to news reports, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) had spent just 0.2 per cent of the environmental compensation it collected from 2018 to 2024 on protecting the environment.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following is **not** correct regarding
Light Pollution?

options.a:
It directly interferes with the circadian rhythms
of animals.
options.b:
It can affect the mating patterns of insects,
leading to a decline in their population.
options.c:
It is recognised as a pollutant under the Air
(Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.
options.d:
LED bulbs contribute more to light pollution
than conventional sodium lamps.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Light pollution,** or **Artificial light at night (ALAN),**  is the **human-made alteration of outdoor light** **levels** from those occurring naturally.
● It refers to the **unwanted or excessive artificial light** and a form of **waste energy** that can cause adverse effects and degrade environmental quality.
○ Being exposed to artificial light at night **reduces the production of melatonin,** popularly called “the hormone of darkness” since it is produced at night. Insufficient melatonin, in turn, disrupts the body’s **circadian rhythm**, the biological clock regulating the sleep-wake cycle. **So, option (a)** **is correct.**
● Light pollution can **disrupt the mating patterns of insects**, potentially leading to population declines. Light pollution can interfere with insects' ability to find mates, lay eggs, and can even make them more vulnerable to predators. **So, option (b) is correct.**
● Monochromatic light **scatters more easily in the atmosphere, when compared to** sodium vapor lamps,
which scatter less and emit primarily in the yellow-orange spectrum. Hence, monochromatic LED bulbs **contribute more to light pollution** than conventional sodium lamps. **So, option (d) is correct.**
● **Stray light** is the unintended, unwanted light that interferes with sensors in an optical system.
● The **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** defines air pollution as **“presence in the** **atmosphere of any air pollutant”** where air pollutant is defined as **any solid, liquid or gaseous** **substance** including noise present in the atmosphere in such a concentration which tends to be injurious to human beings, other living organisms, plants, property or environment.
● Hence, stray lighting is **not recognised** as a pollutant under the act. **So, option (c) is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Monochromatic light is a type of light that consists of a **single wavelength or frequency.**
● It is the **opposite of white light**, which is composed of a mixture of all visible wavelengths.
● Monochromatic light can be produced by a variety of sources, including lasers, LEDs, and certain types of filters.
**Relevance**: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) recently submitted before the National Green Tribunal (NGT), that there is no statutory framework in India that regulates light pollution.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IT, Computers, Space, NanoTech, BioTech, Robotics, Defence, Nuclear, General Science
questions.questionText:
Which of the following technologies can be used for
passive cooling in buildings?
I. - Adding insulators to the building envelope
II. 	 Blue pavementing on rooftops
III. 	 Clustered construction of buildings
IV. 	 Higher ceilings with openings near the roof
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Passive cooling involves **improving indoor thermal comfort** in a building with **little to no dependence** **on ‘man-made’ energy**. Passive cooling techniques help achieve both, reduced demand for cooling as well as lower energy consumption for cooling. Passive cooling strategies revolve around **two primary** **principles**. The first is **controlling heat ingress** in a building. The second is by **dissipating heat** gained by a building.
● **Conduction** is the heat transfer between solids having different temperatures when they are in direct contact with each other. Passive cooling techniques respond to conduction by either resisting or delaying the heat flow.
● **Adding insulation to the building envelope** impedes the heat flow. This could be in the form of glass wool, fibre wool or even air cavities, which in turn help in passive cooling. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Blue pavementing on rooftops, or blue roofs,** are systems that are designed to provide stormwater detention. It is a type of flat roof designed to **temporarily store rainwater** and then slowly release it, helping to manage stormwater runoff and potentially reduce the risk of flooding. Also, blue surfaces absorb more heat than lighter colors. Hence, blue pavementing on rooftops is **not used for passive cooling** in buildings. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Convection** is the heat transfer through a fluid medium such as air or water. Air within the building interior as well as within air gaps in the building envelope, rises as it heats and settles down as it cools. Passive cooling techniques respond to convection by effectively removing the hot air that enters the interior space.
● **Clustered construction of buildings** can reduce direct sun exposure and create **mutual shading to each** **other**, lowering ambient temperature. **So, point III is correct.**
● Preventing heat gain through convection involves **removing the hot air within building interiors.** **Higher ceilings coupled with openings** near or in the roof would allow hot air entering the building interiors to rise and exit through the vents, leading to passive cooling. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Passive cooling techniques respond to **conduction** by either resisting or delaying the heat flow.
● Passive cooling techniques respond to **convection** by effectively removing the hot air that enters the interior space.
● Passive cooling techniques respond to **radiation** by reducing or protecting the openings that allow in hot air.
**Relevance**: In recent times, passive cooling in buildings has been adopted to reduce indoor temperatures without using mechanical energy.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to
Coal Bed Methane (CBM):
I. - It is produced at the same time as the formation of the overlying coal.
II. 	 Its extraction requires drilling wells into the
coal seams and reducing hydrostatic pressure.
III. 	 Unlike coal, CBM does not emit sulphur
dioxide or nitrogen dioxide during combustion.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Coal Bed Methane (CBM) is an **unconventional source of natural gas** that is produced from coal beds.
● CBM is produced as organic material and **turned into coal**. It is then stored on the many surfaces of the coal.
● It is composed mostly of **methane**, with small amounts of nitrogen, carbon dioxide, and heavier hydrocarbons like ethane. Coalbed methane is **“sweet”** rather than “sour”, because it does not contain **hydrogen sulphide.**
● CBM forms at the **same time coal forms**. As organic material dies and is deposited in swamps or swampy lakes, the material undergoes **bacterial and chemical changes** to create **peat deposits**. Over millions of years, this peat gets buried under many layers of sediment as the pressure and temperature of the peat increases.
● Gradually, the peat turns into **lignite or brown coal**, then sub-bituminous coal, bituminous coal, and finally hard **anthracite coal**. While coal is being formed, the decomposing organic material produces **methane gas** - the main component of natural gas - along with nitrogen and carbon dioxide.
● With the pressure of being buried under sediment, most of the methane stays trapped on the surface of the coal. **So, statement I is correct.**
● In coal mines, the CBM is usually held in place by **water pressure**. Extraction requires drilling wells into the coal seams and removing water contained in the seam to reduce hydrostatic pressure and release **adsorbed** (and free) gas out of the coal. **So, statement II is correct.**
● CBM combustion produces significantly lower levels of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen dioxide compared to coal. CBM combustion primarily releases methane and carbon dioxide, with trace amounts of other gases.
● However, the levels of sulfur and nitrogen compounds in CBM are typically very low, leading to minimal emissions of these pollutants during combustion. Hence, combustion of **CBM emits sulphur dioxide or** **nitrogen dioxide**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● India has the **fifth largest** proven coal reserves in the world and thus holds significant prospects for exploration and exploitation of CBM.
● The **Gondwana sediments of eastern India** host the bulk of India’s coal reserves and all the current CBM producing blocks.
● The vast majority of the best prospective areas for CBM development are in eastern India, situated in **Damodar Koel valley and Son valley**.
● CBM projects exist in Raniganj South, Raniganj East and Raniganj North areas in the Raniganj coalfield, the Parbatpur block in Jharia coalfield and the East and west Bokaro coalfields. Son valley includes the Sonhat North and Sohagpur East and West blocks.
**Relevance**: According to the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas, India aims to increase Coal Bed Methane (CBM) production to 5.0 million metric standard cubic meters per day (MMSCMD) by 2027-28.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Biodiversity Beyond National Jurisdiction (BBNJ) Agreement:
I. - It is legally binding on all its signatories.
II. 	 It is a part of the United Nations Convention on
Law of the Sea (UNCLOS).
III. 	 It exempts fisheries and non-living resources
from its purview.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India recently signed **the** **Biodiversity Beyond National Jurisdiction (BBNJ) Agreement, or the** **"Treaty of the High Seas".** The goal of the BBNJ Agreement is to protect marine life in areas of the ocean that are **not owned by any one country**. These areas start 370 kilometres from any country's coastline and cover two-thirds of the world's oceans.
○ Often referred to as the **‘High Seas’,** areas beyond national jurisdiction are the **global common** oceans open to all for internationally lawful purposes such as navigation, overflight, laying submarine cables and pipelines, etc.
● This agreement also helps meet the goals of **Sustainable Development, especially SDG 14,** which focuses on protecting life underwater.  It **bans harmful activities**, such as destructive fishing practices and pollution, in the high seas. This agreement also supports the target of protecting 30 per cent of the world's land and sea by 2030, which was set during the **Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity** **Framework** meeting in 2022.
● It aims to address the growing concerns over the long-term protection of marine biodiversity in the high seas. It sets precise mechanisms for the **sustainable use of marine biological diversity** through international cooperation and coordination.
● The BBNJ Agreement was agreed upon in March 2023 and is open for signature for two years starting September 2023.
● It will be an international **legally binding** treaty after it enters force **120 days after the 60****th** **ratification,** **acceptance, approval or accession.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● The BBNJ agreement is an international treaty under the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the** **Sea (UNCLOS).** **So, statement II is correct.**
● Parties **cannot claim or exercise sovereign rights** over marine resources derived from the high seas and ensure fair and equitable sharing of benefits. It follows an **inclusive, integrated, ecosystem-centric** **approach** based on the precautionary principle and promotes using traditional knowledge and the best available scientific knowledge.
● The BBNJ agreement regulates conservation and sustainable use of marine biological materials, meaning **living organisms**. It **does not apply to non-living resources** such as oil and gas in the international seabed and water column and other resources such **as fisheries. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The BBNJ Agreement will be the **third implementation agreement** under UNCLOS if and when it enters into force, alongside its sister implementation agreements: **the 1994 Part XI** **Implementation Agreement** (which addresses the exploration and extraction of mineral resources in the international seabed area) and **the 1995 UN Fish Stocks Agreement** (which addresses the conservation and management of straddling and highly migratory fish stocks).
● **UNCLOS** was adopted on December 10, 1982, and came into force on November 16, 1994. It is crucial for the environmental protection of the seas and addressing maritime boundaries, rights to marine resources, and dispute resolution.
● It establishes the **International Seabed Authority** to regulate mining and related activities on the ocean floor beyond national jurisdiction.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006:
I. 	 It recognizes community rights only and not
individual rights.
II. 	 The title for Community Forest Rights is
vested in the Gram Sabha.
III. 	No forest rights can be granted within Critical
Wildlife Habitats.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition Of Forest Rights) Act,** **2006,** also known as, **the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006,** aims to recognise and vest the **forest rights** **and occupation in forest land** in forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers who have been residing in such forests for generations but whose rights could not be recorded; to provide for a framework for recording the forest rights so vested and the nature of evidence required for such recognition and vesting in respect of forest land.
● The Act encompasses **Rights of Self-cultivation and Habitation** which are usually regarded as **Individual rights**; and **Community Rights** as Grazing, Fishing and access to Water bodies in forests, Habitat Rights for PVTGs, Traditional Seasonal Resource access of Nomadic and Pastoral community, access to biodiversity, community right to intellectual property and traditional knowledge, recognition of traditional customary rights and right to protect, regenerate or conserve or manage any community forest resource for sustainable use. Thus, the Act recognizes **both individual and community rights**. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● The Act also provides rights to **allocation of forest land for developmental purposes** to fulfil basic infrastructural needs of the community. In conjunction with the Right to Fair Compensation and
Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Settlement Act, 2013 FRA protects the tribal population from eviction without rehabilitation and settlement.
● The Act enjoins upon the **Gram Sabha** and rights holders the responsibility of conservation and protection of bio-diversity, wildlife, forests, adjoining catchment areas, water sources and other ecologically sensitive areas as well as to stop any destructive practices affecting these resources or cultural and natural heritage of the tribals.
● The Gram Sabha is also a **highly empowered body** under the Act, enabling the tribal population to have a decisive say in the determination of local policies and schemes impacting them.
● Gram Sabha is the authority to initiate the process for determining the nature and extent of individual or community rights. The **title for Community Forest Rights** **is also vested in the Gram Sabha**. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Critical Wildlife Habitats (CWHs) are **areas inside wildlife sanctuaries, national parks and tiger** **reserves** – known as **‘protected areas’** – where people’s activities like cattle grazing or collecting leaves compete with the needs of wildlife.
● The Act defines CWHs as areas that are **“required to be kept as inviolate for the purposes of wildlife** **conservation”,** meaning no forest rights can be granted within them. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: A two-page letter regarding the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, issued from the office of the Principal Chief Conservator of Forests (HoFF), Himachal Pradesh, Sameer Rastogi, became a dispute between local activists and environmentalists in the state.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the iron and steel industry:
I. 	 It contributes to more than 5% of global
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
II. 	 India is the world’s second-largest producer
of crude steel.
III. 	Coke serves both as a reagent and as a heat
source in the steel manufacturing process.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The iron and steel industry is the **largest coal consumer** and the most greenhouse gas intensive industry. The iron and steel sector emits 2.6 Gt CO2e annually, which is **7% of the global emissions** from energy use and **7–9% of global anthropogenic CO****2** **emissions**—the highest among heavy industries. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **India became the world’s second-largest steel producer in 2018 surpassing Japan** and remained so since then. In 2023, India produced 141 million tonnes of crude steel out of the world production of 1892 million tonnes.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Coke** plays an indispensable role in the steelmaking process, primarily in blast furnaces, where it serves as a **reducing agent** where coke reacts with iron ore to extract iron, leaving behind molten metal. Coke also acts as a **heat source,** where the combustion of coke generates the extreme temperatures required for smelting iron. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: According to the May 2025 Pedal to the Metal report by Global Energy Monitor (GEM), India has emerged as the world’s largest developer of steel capacity, accounting for 40 per cent of global capacity.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to
agricultural and allied sector in India:
Revolution Related to
I. - Yellow Oilseeds
II. - Silver Eggs and poultry
III. - Pink Fertilizer
IV. - Purple Aromatic crops
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India achieved significant growth in agriculture, milk, fish, oilseeds, and fruits, and vegetables due to green, white, blue and yellow revolutions. All these revolutions have brought prosperity for the farmers.
● The concept of **Rainbow revolution** is an **integrated development** of crop cultivation, horticulture, forestry, fishery, poultry, animal husbandry, and food processing industry. The concept of Rainbow revolution in agriculture is a step towards sustainability.
● The multiple colors of the Rainbow Revolution indicate multiple farm practices.
**Revolution** **Related to** Green Revolution Foodgrains White Revolution Milk **Yellow Revolution** **Oilseeds** Blue Revolution Fisheries Golden Revolution Fruits **Silver Revolution** **Eggs and poultry** Round Revolution Potatoes **Pink Revolution** **Meat and onion/prawn** Grey Revolution Fertilizers Red Revolution Tomatoes and meat Brown Revolution Leather **Purple Revolution (or) Lavender Revolution** **Aromatic crops**
● **So, pairs I, II and IV are correctly matched and pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Agricultural scientist M.S.Swaminathan, popularly known as the “father of the green revolution” in India, passed away recently.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.microTopic: Allied Sectors (Animal Husbandry, Horticulture, Sericulture, Apiculture, Aquaculture)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to floriculture:
I. 	 India is the largest producer of floricultural
products in the world.
II. 	 Himachal Pradesh is the largest producer of
floricultural products in India.
III. 	Floriculture has a lower water intensity and
a shorter growing period than conventional
cereal crops.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Floriculture, or flower farming**, is a subset of horticulture and is essentially concerned with development and cultivation of **flowering and ornamental plants** for gardens and for floristry, comprising the floral commerce.
● The development of new varieties via plant breeding is a major occupation of floriculturists. The flower industry comprises cultivation and trade of flowers, foliage, potted plants and bedding plants. The cutflower trade comprises rose, chrysanthemum, gladiolus, carnation and lilies.
● **China** is the largest producer of floricultural products in the world. After China, **India** is the world's **second-largest producer** of flowers. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Floriculture is now commercially cultivated in several states with **Tamil Nadu (21%),** Karnataka (16%), Madhya Pradesh (14%) and West Bengal (12%), having gone ahead of other producing states like Mizoram, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Orissa, Jharkhand, Haryana, Assam and Chhattisgarh.
● Hence, **Tamil Nadu** is the top producer of floricultural products like flowers, aromatics, and medicinal plants in India. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Floriculture has a lower water requirement and a shorter growing cycle compared to many** **conventional cereal crops,** due to differences in their growth cycle, cultivation methods (often protected), and the physiological characteristics of the plants.  **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● India's floriculture sector has expanded dramatically in the last several years.
● The **major importing countries** of India’s floriculture products were U.S.A, Netherland, United Arab
Emirates, U.K and Canada.
● There are more than 300 export-oriented units in India.
● More than 50% of the floriculture units are based in **Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu.**
● With the technical collaborations from foreign companies, the Indian floriculture industry is poised to
increase its share in world trade.
**Relevance**: In 2023-24, India exported just 19,678 tonnes of floricultural products, worth ₹717.83 crore ($86.63 million), amounting to 0.6 per cent of international trade in flowers.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Millets:
**Statement I:**
Millets utilize water and soil nutrients more
efficiently than cereals like wheat and rice.
**Statement II:**
Millets follow the C4 photosynthetic pathway,
whereas wheat and rice rely on the C3 pathway.
**Statement III:**
Millets naturally contain very low levels of amino
acids.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Millets are **small-grained nutritious minor cereal crops** that are resistant to different abiotic stresses resulting from climate change. Millets belong to the **Poaceae family** and are grown for its edible seeds, similar to more common cereals like wheat and rice.
● Millets are considered **more nutritious than common cereals** like rice and wheat. They contain higher amounts of **protein, fiber, and micronutrients**. Their high fiber content helps in better digestion and blood sugar management.
● Examples of millets include; Sorghum Millet (Jowar), Proso Millet (Chena / Barri), Pearl Millet (Bajra), Foxtail Millet (Kakum / Kangni), Finger Millet (Ragi), Browntop Millet (Korle), Barnyard Millet (Sanwa), Little Millet (Moraiyo), Buckwheat Millet (Kuttu), Amaranth Millet (Rajgira), Kodo Millet, etc.
● While cereals like rice and wheat require specific climates and abundant water, millets can be grown in **diverse environments, including dry and less fertile soils**. This makes them more versatile and sustainable compared to the traditional cereals. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Millets utilize the **C4 photosynthetic pathway**, which is more efficient in high-temperature, lowmoisture, and high-light environments. In contrast, crops like **wheat and rice** follow the **C3 pathway,**
which is less efficient under such stress conditions and more prone to photorespiration. This physiological advantage makes millets more **resilient to climate variability** and better suited for cultivation in **arid** **and semi-arid regions. So, statement II is correct.**
● Millet protein contains a **higher amount** of essential and non-essential amino acids including leucine, phenylalanine, **proline**, serine, tyrosine, aspartic and glutamic acid. **So, statement III is not correct.** **So, only one of the statements II and III is correct and that explains statement I.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Plants have evolved **three types of photosynthetic pathways** in response to distinct environmental conditions: **C****3****, C****4****, and crassulacean acid metabolism (CAM).**
● **The Calvin cycle** is usually divided into three phases, namely **carboxylation, reduction and** **regeneration**. It is also known as the **C3 cycle** (first stable product is a three-carbon acid, 3PGA) or the photosynthetic carbon reduction cycle because CO2 is reduced.
● The complete cycle has thirteen enzyme-catalysed reactions of which only three are unique to the pathway. These enzymes are localised in the chloroplast stroma. Plants that fix carbon exclusively by the Calvin cycle are called C3 plants.
● Approximately **85% of plant species use the C****3** photosynthetic pathway whereby CO2 is fixed by Ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (Rubisco) in the Calvin cycle, generating the three-carbon compound glycerate-3-phosphate.
● In contrast to C3 plants, species with **C****4** **photosynthesis** overcome this limitation by **compartmentalization of carbon fixation** and the Calvin cycle in mesophyll and bundle sheath (BS) cells, respectively.
● This allows C4 photosynthesis to use a biochemical CO2-concentrating mechanism that enhances the operating efficiency of Rubisco and inhibits ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP) oxygenation and associated photorespiratory losses. **Relevance**: In an effort to provide sustainable income and enhance the livelihood of tribal farmers engaged in the cultivation of millets, the Tamil Nadu government has recently announced the Tribal Farmers Development Scheme.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I:** Indian mustard oil is less marketable in consumer markets due to its strong pungency, leading to a preference for alternatives like Canola oil. **Statement-II:** Indian mustard contains a higher concentration of glucosinolates compared to Canola mustard varieties.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Indian mustard oi**l contains sulfur and nitrogen containing compounds that give the oil its characteristic pungent smell and strong flavour. While this is traditional in Indian cuisine, many consumers prefer cooking oils with a milder taste and odour. As a result, the **pungency limits its marketability, especially** **in consumer segments where neutral oils like Canola are preferred.**
○ **Canola oil,** bred from low-glucosinolate mustard varieties, is more widely accepted because of its **bland taste and better sensory appeal, making it a more marketable alternative. So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Traditional **Indian mustard (Brassica juncea) contains high glucosinolate levels** of around 120–130 ppm in its seeds**, significantly higher than t**he less than 30 ppm found in **Canola-quality varieties.** This **pungent taste arises from the high concentration of glucosinolates** naturally present in Indian mustard seeds whereas Canola varieties have much lower levels. These glucosinolates are sulfur- and nitrogencontaining compounds that are responsible for the sharp flavour and smell in mustard oil. **So, statement** **II is correct.** **So,** both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
**Relevance:** Rapeseed-mustard oil is the third-largest edible oil consumed in India. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Sugarcane cultivation requires an optimal gradient of soil humidity.
**Statement II:** The vegetative growth of sugarcane is directly proportional to the amount of evapotranspiration. **Statement III:** The reproductive part of the sugarcane plant
is more commercially valuable than its nonreproductive parts.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sugarcane is a tropical–subtropical cash crop** that requires warm temperatures, abundant sunlight, and adequate moisture for optimal growth. It is cultivated primarily for its vegetative parts — the stalks —
which store sucrose, the main commercial product. Soil moisture management and water use efficiency are critical for yield and sugar content.
● Sugarcane thrives best when the **soil has consistent moisture availability** throughout the root zone, allowing for a **moisture gradient that encourages root penetration and nutrient uptake.** Optimal soil humidity gradients prevent moisture stress and support steady growth. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The vegetative growth of sugarcane is directly proportional to the amount of evapotranspiration.** This relationship underscores the importance of maintaining adequate soil moisture throughout the crop's growth period, as sufficient water availability ensures that the plant can effectively replace the water lost. When this balance is maintained, optimal vegetative development occurs, which is essential for achieving high yields in sugarcane cultivation. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Sugarcane is cultivated for its stalks (non reproductive part),** which store sucrose in their parenchyma tissues. The **reproductive structures (flowers and seeds) are generally undesirable in commercial** **cultivation** because flowering signals maturity and diverts energy away from sugar storage, reducing yield and quality**. So, statement III is not correct.** **So, only one of the Statements-II and III is correct and that explains Statement-I.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Tropical Sugarcane**
● **States**: Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Goa, Kerala, Puducherry.
● **Share in production**: ~55% of India’s cane output.
● **Average yield**: ~80 tonnes/ha.
● **Climate**: Equitable, long growing season (12–18 months), fewer weather extremes.
● **Advantages**: ○ Long sunshine hours, cool nights → higher sugar accumulation. ○ Better irrigation and package of practices. ○ Higher sugar recovery (Maharashtra, Karnataka, Gujarat).
● **Constraints**: ○ Moisture stress in March–June in non-irrigated areas. ○ Pests/diseases like red rot. ○ Poor ratoon management in some areas. ○ Inadequate quality seed availability. **Sub-tropical Sugarcane**
● **States**: Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Haryana, Punjab.
● **Share in production**: ~45% of India’s cane output.
● **Average yield**: ~60 tonnes/ha.
● **Climate**: Extremes—very hot summers (Apr–Jun), heavy monsoon (Jul–Oct), cold winters with frost (Dec–Jan).
● **Advantages**: ○ Large cultivation area (UP is largest sugarcane-growing state).
● **Constraints**: ○ Shorter growing season. ○ High temperature disparity; frost damage in winters. ○ Water logging, floods, and moisture stress. ○ Poor ratoon performance. ○ Lack of early-maturing, high-sucrose varieties. ○ Late planting and pest/disease problems. ○ Imbalanced fertilizer use.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following crops:
I. 	 Cashew
II. 	 Coffee
III. 	Cotton
IV. 	 Rubber
Which of the above are commonly cultivated in
regions with laterite soil?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Laterite soil** is a distinct type of soil formed through intense and prolonged tropical **weathering** in hot and humid climates. It is **rich in iron and aluminium oxides and typically red in color** due to the presence of iron oxides. While chemically rich in certain minerals, it is poor in essential nutrients like potash, lime, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen. This soil is primarily found in regions of India receiving high rainfall, such as the Western and Eastern Ghats, parts of Maharashtra, Odisha, West Bengal, and northeastern states.
● **Cashew is well-suited for cultivation in laterite soils.** This crop thrives in acidic and well-drained conditions, both of which are characteristic features of laterite soil. The states of Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala have successfully utilized lateritic regions for extensive cashew nut production. The porosity and drainage capacity of laterite soil, coupled with its iron-rich composition, support the optimal growth of cashew trees, especially in coastal and dryland areas where this soil type is abundant. **So, point** **I is correct.**
● **Coffee** **adapts exceptionally well to laterite soil**, particularly in the high-rainfall hilly regions of Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu. Despite being low in fertility, with regular organic enrichment and soil conservation practices, laterite soil provides the right conditions for coffee cultivation. In regions like Coorg and the Western Ghats, coffee plantations are a major agricultural activity on lateritic slopes. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● **Cotton is not ideally suited for cultivation in laterite soil due to the soil’s inherent limitations.** Cotton generally requires deep, well-drained, and nutrient-rich soils, with a preference for black soil (regur) or loamy soil that can retain moisture and supply essential nutrients like nitrogen, potassium, and phosphorus**.** **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Rubber cultivation is highly compatible with laterite soil, especially in tropical regions with high** **rainfall like Kerala and parts of Karnataka. T**he well-drained and porous nature of laterite soil, along with adequate moisture from rainfall, creates favorable conditions for rubber trees. The resilience of rubber trees to acidic conditions also adds to the suitability of this crop for laterite soils. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following serve as primary substrates
for the production of biofuels?
I. 	 Cellulose
II. 	 Hemicellulose
III. 	Lignin
IV. 	 Tryptophan
V. 	 Vitamin B12
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.b: I, III, IV, and V only
options.d: I, II, III, IV, and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Biofuels are renewable energy sources made from plant biomass, aiming to reduce reliance on fossil fuels. Key plant cell wall components -  cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin play different roles in biofuel production.
● **Cellulose** is a major source of fermentable sugars used to produce biofuels like ethanol. It is abundant and a **primary substrate for biofuel production** after enzymatic breakdown. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Hemicellulose** also provides sugars for biofuel production and is easier to break down than cellulose, making it an **important substrate in biofuel processe**s. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Lignin,** while not directly fermentable, can be converted into various chemicals that can be used in biofuels or serve as precursors for biofuel production**. Therefore, it is also a substrate for Biofuel** **production. So, point III is correct.**
● **Tryptophan is an amino acid, not a structural component** of plant cell walls, and is not typically used for biofuel production. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Vitamin B12 is a complex vitamin, not a structural component** of plant cell walls, and is not typically used for biofuel production. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**    E20 fuel blend sparked off a debate.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following crops in increasing order of
their water footprint:
I. 	 Maize
II. 	 Wheat
III. 	Rice
IV. 	 Orange
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The water footprint** of a crop refers to the total volume of freshwater used to produce one unit (usually 1 kg) of that crop, including water consumed in irrigation, soil moisture, and processing.
● **Maize** has a **global average water footprint of about 900 litres per kg**, making it relatively waterefficient compared to wheat and rice. Its C4 photosynthetic pathway allows higher productivity per unit water, especially in warm climates. This makes maize a preferred crop in semi-arid regions where water conservation is critical.
● **Wheat** has a **water footprint of around 1,300 litres per kg**, higher than maize but much lower than rice. Wheat is generally grown in temperate and sub-tropical regions, often requiring irrigation during dry spells. Its water use depends on variety, sowing season, and rainfall availability.
● **Rice i**s a water-intensive crop with a **water footprint of about 3,400 litres per kg**. This high requirement is mainly due to paddy cultivation methods that involve continuous flooding of fields, which increases evapotranspiration. While traditional paddy uses excessive water, modern water-saving methods like **System of Rice Intensification (SRI)** can reduce this footprint.
● Oranges have a **water footprint of around 460 litres per kg**, the lowest among the four crops listed. As a perennial fruit tree, oranges use water more efficiently over their long lifespan, with irrigation mainly during dry spells. Their water requirement is much lower than staple cereals. **So, the correct ascending order is - IV - I - II - III.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements about
phosphorus as a nutrient fertilizer are correct?
I. 	 It is a relatively immobile element in soils.
II. 	 It plays a key role in root initiation in
flowering plants.
III. 	In plants, phosphorus is mainly found in
tissues with high energy demand.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Phosphorus (P)** is one of the three major macronutrients essential for plant growth, along with nitrogen and potassium. It is required in relatively small amounts compared to nitrogen but plays a critical role in plant metabolism, energy transfer, and structural functions.
● **Phosphorus moves very little in the soil i.e relatively immobile compared to other nutrients like** **nitrate or sulphate.** Once phosphate fertilizers are applied, phosphorus quickly reacts with soil minerals—such as calcium—forming compounds that are less available to plants. This limited movement means phosphorus must be placed close to plant roots for effective uptake. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Phosphorus is essential for early plant development, particularly root initiation and growth.** Adequate phosphorus supply promotes the formation of a strong and extensive root system, enabling better water and nutrient absorption. In flowering plants, early phosphorus application helps seedlings establish more effectively, especially in cold or compacted soils where root growth is otherwise restricted. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **Phosphorus** is highly mobile within the plant and **is translocated to young, actively growing tissues** **and later to fruiting areas. These regions have high energy requirements** for processes like seed and fruit development. Since phosphorus is a key component of ATP and other energy molecules, it is concentrated in tissues with high metabolic activity. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Which of the following crops have the ability to
enhance the nitrogen content of the soil?
I. 	 Pearl Millet
II. 	 Finger Millet
III. 	Rice
IV. 	 Pea plant
V. 	 Sunflower
VI.	 Guar
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.c: I, II, IV, and V only
options.d: II, III, IV, V and VI only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pearl millet (****Pennisetum glaucum****) is a cereal crop commonly grown in arid and semi-arid regions.** While it is drought-resistant and nutritious, it does not have the biological capability to fix atmospheric nitrogen. Being a non-leguminous plant, it lacks the symbiotic association with nitrogen-fixing bacteria like Rhizobium. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Finger millet (****Eleusine coracana****) is a cereal crop** known for its resilience and high nutritional value, particularly in terms of calcium content**. However, similar to other millets, it does not fix nitrogen from** **the atmosphere. So, point II is not correct.**
● **Rice (****Oryza sativa****) is a staple cereal crop that grows predominantly in flooded conditions.** While some traditional and organic farming methods may use nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria (such as Anabaena in association with Azolla), **rice itself is not capable of fixing nitrogen. So, point III is not correct.**
● **The Pea plant (****Pisum sativum****) is a leguminous crop and is well known for its ability to enhance** **nitrogen levels in the soil.** It forms a symbiotic relationship with Rhizobium bacteria, which colonize the root nodules of the plant. These bacteria convert atmospheric nitrogen into a form that the plant can use, while also enriching the surrounding soil with nitrogen, benefiting future crops planted in the same area. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Sunflower (****Helianthus annuus****)** is a non-leguminous oilseed crop. Although it has a deep root system that can help improve soil structure and access nutrients from deeper layers, **it does not contribute to** **nitrogen fixation. Without a symbiotic nitrogen-fixing mechanism, sunflowers do not increase the** **nitrogen content of the soil. So, point V is not correct.**
● **Guar (****Cyamopsis tetragonoloba****),** also known as cluster bean, **is a leguminous plant that has the ability** **to fix atmospheric nitrogen** through its root nodules in association with nitrogen-fixing bacteria**. Guar** **is commonly grown in rotation with other crops to naturally replenish soil nitrogen levels.** . **So, point** **VI is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Q78**
**Answer: a**
**Explanation:**
● **Soil salinization refers to the accumulation of soluble salts (such as sodium chloride, sodium sulfate,** **and magnesium salts) in the soil** to levels that negatively affect plant growth and soil health. It is a major environmental and agricultural issue in arid and semi-arid regions, often caused by poor irrigation practices, rising water tables, or seawater intrusion.
● **Soil salinization is increasingly recognized as a serious threat to global food security due to its** **widespread and escalating impact on agricultural productivity.** This phenomenon occurs when watersoluble salts such as sodium, potassium, magnesium, chlorides, and carbonates accumulate excessively in the soil, degrading its fertility and structure. The Global Status of Salt-Affected Soils report was presented
by FAO. The report estimates the area of salt-affected soils at 1 381 million ha (Mha), or 10.7 percent of the total global land area. This directly threatens the food production capacity of approximately 1.5 billion people. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Soil salinization significantly increases the soil's susceptibility to erosion due to its detrimental** **effects on soil structure, fertility, and biological activity**. As soluble salts such as sodium accumulate either naturally through parent rock material and groundwater infiltration, or due to human activities like poor irrigation practices, deforestation, and excessive use of agrochemicals; the soil begins to degrade. High concentrations of salts, especially sodium, disrupt the soil's physical integrity, weakening the bonds between soil particles. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Soil salinization significantly reduces the permeability of the soil, which in turn hinders water** **infiltration and limits groundwater recharge.** As soluble salts, particularly sodium, accumulate in the deeper soil layers, they cause soil particles to disperse and compact, making the soil denser and less porous. This compaction restricts the downward movement of water, leading to poor infiltration and increased surface runoff. As a result, less water reaches the groundwater table, affecting its natural replenishment. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both Statement-II and Statement-III are correct and both of them explain Statement-I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Soil degradation has a close link with water logging and salinity problems. Irrigation salinity has a major impact on dryland crops.
